data.title: Alphatec Holdings: Backing Improves
data.description:
Over the summer, I concluded that Alphatec Holdings, Inc. (ATEC) was delivering on continued growth while narrowing losses. While this was to be applauded, I feared persistent losses and continued dilution as a key risk to investors.

Focus on growth of the business had come at the expense of shareholder value, amidst debt taken on and dilution taking place. While innovation and growth were seen, true profitability remained elusive, and despite a reasonable 3 times sales multiple, I erred on the side of caution.

This was followed by strong momentum in the second half of 2025. Following a soft, or conservative, outlook for 2026, a small setback has been observed here. With my appreciation for Alphatec growing, current valuations are not compelling enough to buy the dip just yet, as I am keen to keep a closer eye on the shares here.

All About The Spine

Alphatec aims to revolutionize spine surgery by creating clinical distinction and driving surgeon adoption, all designed to gain a greater market share of an already growing market. Part of its mission is not just to provide better spine solutions, but also to address its greatest challenge, that of lack of durability.

The company specifically cites a competitive advantage versus larger and more diversified global medical peers like Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Medtronic (MDT) and Globus Medical (GMED) by being purely focused on the spine.

The company focuses on a wide range of products used for pre-intra and post-operations. Products include imaging, implants, and monitoring, used in a wide variety of procedures and approaches, all supported by the EOS insight platform of the business.

Alphatec went public at $9 per share in 2006, yet that does not tell much given a massive reverse stock split taking place ever since, with shares going public at >$100 per share if we factor that in. Shares fell to the $1 mark in the 2010s as a $100 million business at the time was posting substantial losses.

A complete strategic overhaul to focus on growth, focus, and margin components sent shares trading up to the $15 mark in 2021-2024. However, towards the end of 2024, shares were back to just $5 per share before momentum reappeared in 2025.

Managing Growth

The strategic reset made 2022 sales rise in a spectacular fashion, reported up 44% to $351 million. This, however, was accompanied by a huge operating loss of $147 million.

Revenues rose by another 37% to $482 million in 2023, as operating losses rose further to $173 million, even as modest gains have been seen on a relative basis here.

Revenues rose by another 27% to $612 million in 2024, as operating losses narrowed to $136 million, with some real progress on the bottom line made, yet still large losses being reported.

With some stagnation seen on the margin front in the first quarter of 2025, shares traded at $11 over the summer, with 146 million shares valuing equity at $1.6 billion. This, however, excluded about half a billion in net debt load, for a $2.1 billion enterprise valuation, equal to about 3 times sales guidance at $734 million for the year.

Having been skeptical of Alphatec in various cases before, I acknowledged that some trends were turning positive, including positive cash flows, although these are not the same as profits, among others, adjusted for quite heavy stock-based compensation being doled out.

It was more the willingness and ease with which management was willing to dilute shareholders that made me cautious and detrimental down the road.

Making Progress

Since the summer, shares have made real inroads, doubling to $23 towards year-end 2025, as a setback early in 2026 set shares back to the $18 mark, still marking massive returns since the summer.

Second-quarter sales rose by 28% to more than $185 million, as GAAP operating losses narrowed to $13 million, marking $22 million in gains on that front, with those numbers even impacted by amortization charges and some smaller litigation charges as well. Driven by the strong momentum, the full year's sales guidance was hiked by eight million to $742 million.

Third-quarter sales rose by more than 30% to nearly $197 million as GAAP reporting losses ticked up to $16 million, which is an increase on a sequential basis, while marking a dramatic improvement versus the year before. The sequential reduction came after litigation & amortization charges rose quickly to over $10 million this quarter. If adjusted for, shows that realistic operating losses are rapidly approaching the breakeven mark. That, however, is all ahead of taxes and substantial cash expenses due on a nearly half-a-billion net debt load.

The company hiked the full-year sales guidance to $760 million, suggesting that fourth-quarter revenues are seen around $209 million. This marks further substantial growth, which is frankly impressive. The good news is that dilution is taking place at a slower pace, with the third quarter share count up just over 5% on the year before. While this obviously is not great, yet amidst 30% topline sales growth, revenue growth on a per-share basis is impressive, while net debt is pretty flat.

Driven by these very impressive numbers, shares have been re-rated higher to nearly $23 towards year-end, now having pulled back to $18 per share.

Starting 2026 Soft

Shares of Alphatec suffered a setback at the start of 2026 as the company provided preliminary numbers and issued its 2026 outlook. Fourth-quarter revenues are seen near $213 million, all a bit stronger than originally guided for. So far, so good.

The issue was more seen with the 2026 outlook, calling for about 17% sales growth, with revenues seen around $890 million. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $130 million, which compares to a $91 million number on this front in 2025, and while this marks further progress, it is a bit less than investors anticipated after expectations ran hot.

With 151 million shares trading at $18, the market value of Alphatec has risen to just over $2.7 billion, for a near $3.2 billion enterprise valuation. Based on the $764 million in sales expected in 2025, the business trades just over 4 times sales now. Note that these EBITDA numbers are entirely different from realistic earnings.

Stock-based compensation expenses trend at $70 million here, while cash and non-cash interest expenses trend over $40 million. Furthermore, depreciation expenses trend at $60 million each year as well. This makes it so that realistic earnings are not yet seen, even as cash flow positive numbers are reported; those disregard stock-based compensation expenses and dilution, of course.

And Now?

Having been skeptical of Alphatec in various cases in the past, I am impressed with continued growth and continued operating leverage achieved, although realistic earnings remain elusive here. In between all this, valuation looks pretty reasonable at 4 times sales, given that these sales grow by 30%, yet a slower pace of growth in 2026 and lack of realistic earnings remain an issue as well.

Weighing it altogether, I am more constructive on Alphatec amidst continued growth and operational leverage, yet I am not yet willing to alter my stance entirely. Alphatec remains a neutral play for me, and while my appreciation for the business and its growth has improved meaningfully, I am not buying this latest dip just yet.

data.title: JETS: Don't Underestimate The Long Term Risks Associated With Owning Airline Stocks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) aims to provide broad economic exposure to air travel, though with a significant emphasis on U.S. airlines.

While the airline industry is certainly an exciting one, I do not agree with the fund sponsor's thesis that "the global commercial airline industry represents a highly dynamic investment opportunity".

This is because, although I recognize that airlines have recovered from the massive disruption of the pandemic era, I think it is essential that the long-term risks associated with the industry are not overlooked. Given the low margins and weak balance sheets of most airlines, they seem particularly vulnerable to the increasingly uncertain geopolitical environment which has developed over recent years. I therefore give the fund a strong sell rating.

Holding Selection

Before analyzing the ETF, it's essential to understand how it is put together. The ETF is passive in the sense of following a quant-based selection criteria, reflected in an index which was created solely for the fund.

The selection criteria, as shown above, does have a number of distinct layers.

At quarterly rebalances, the fund buys 10% stakes in each of the four leading U.S. airlines, amounting for 40% of the fund in total. It's important to note that "leading" here is defined as a ranking which balances market cap, trading volumes and to a lesser extent passenger load factor (though the specific formula used by the ETF manager to balance these three factors is not public).

In the next layer, the criteria is expanded to include Canadian airlines too, alongside U.S. airlines. The next eight leading names (using the previous definition) are then given a 3% weighting, representing 24% in total.

The next layers down then use a slightly different selection formula, which rather than emphasizing market cap and dollar volumes traded, instead balances "cash flow return on invested capital (CFROIC) and average dollar value traded with additional inputs based on sales per share growth, gross margins, and sales yield". This formula is applied to eight U.S. and Canadian securities with a 2% weight (16% in total). It's also important to note that from this stage down, it's not just passenger airlines which are covered, but rather a broad selection of businesses relating to airlines (e.g. airport operators, booking platforms and aircraft manufacturers).

The final two stages apply these same quantitative metrics to 10 international companies (with 1% ranking each, so 10% in total), then again to 20 further international companies (with 0.5% each, so another 10%).

Given these rules and their overall complexity, it's worth getting a practical sense what the fund currently owns. The top ten holdings below account for around 61% of the fund's total holdings. Notably, resulting from the first "layer" outlined above, around 44% of the fund is represented by the four largest U.S. airlines (though this resets to 40% at each quarterly rebalance).

Holding Name Allocation
Southwest Airlines Co 11.53%
United Airlines Holdings Inc 11.03%
Delta Air Lines Inc 10.64%
American Airlines Group Inc 10.64%
Allegiant Travel Co 3.19%
Sun Country Airlines Holdings Inc 3.04%
JetBlue Airways Corp 2.91%
Air Canada 2.88%
Alaska Air Group Inc 2.84%
SkyWest Inc 2.68%

While the bulk of investor returns are clearly linked to the results of the big four airlines shown above (Southwest, United, Delta, and American), it's worth understanding the broad categories owned overall.

Based on the full list of holdings available on the fund's webpage, I have compiled a table showing the major categories covered:

Category Percentage Share Number
U.S. Airlines 61.02% 10
Canadian Airlines 2.88% 1
International Airlines 11.63% 19
Airport Operators 3.47% 6
Booking Platforms 7.35% 5
Aircraft/Component Manufacturers 10.04% 6
Other 3.30% 3

While there is a degree of diversification, investors should understand that JETS is overwhelmingly a bet on passenger airlines, currently accounting for more than 75% of the total holdings. It is also a substantial bet on U.S. airlines specifically, since these represent 61% of the fund's value.

Challenging The Bull Case

The fund's investment case gives a number of reasons why investors should have a positive long-term outlook on the airline industry.

One of the reasons stated is that

As economies around the world develop, they generate increasing numbers of middle-class consumers with disposable incomes. This trend is particularly strong in emerging markets such as India and China, where consumers are more likely to spend money on experiences such as travel.

While it is certainly true to an extent, it does seem a stretch to apply this secular tailwind to the JETS fund specifically. This is because, from my point of view, it is not clear that the U.S. airlines which dominate the fund will be able to monetize the middle classes of India and China. While the fund has 0.55% stakes in two Chinese airlines (China Southern and Air China), these seem very unlikely to materially move the needle for the fund's performance.

Another reason which I do not find the investment case convincing is because it overlooks the low margins and weak balance sheets of airlines, which make them particularly vulnerable to geopolitical shocks. For example, we can consider the big four U.S. airlines emphasized in the fund.

Tangible Book Value (millions) Net income margin Levered FCF Margin
American ($10,091) 1.11% 0.78%
Delta $3,101 7.36% 3.27%
Southwest $6,511 1.38% -0.05%
United $7,120 5.64% 3.91%

Particularly notable, and accounting for over 10% of JETS is American Airlines which enjoys net profit margins of just over 1% and has a negative tangible book value of around $10 billion. This weakness is also reflected in its B+ credit rating from Fitch, far into the "junk" bond category formally described as "highly speculative". While the bonds of Delta and Southwest are at least at an investment grade according to the ratings agencies, United's bonds are not considered non-investment grade either, with a BB+ rating from Fitch. This is far from encouraging for long-term investors.

Exogenous Shocks

While airline stocks can perform reasonably well during stable periods, the real risk comes from extended periods of disruption such as we saw in 2020-21.

As shown above, the JETS ETF was even able to outperform the S&P (since inception) until early 2018. As a result of the pandemic, though, the ultimate divergence in performance is pretty stunning. Overall, the S&P offered a total return almost 11 times greater than JETS during the 11 years from inception.

I believe that the market is underpricing the combination of the weak financial profiles of airlines as well as the relatively high probability of disruption to their operations over the next decade. Indeed, even if only considering pandemics, the probability of a serious one taking place over the next 25 years has been estimated at around 50%. Scientists have also suggested that the next pandemic could be worse than COVID. The effect of this on the broad airline industry would likely be catastrophic, as already evidenced by the fund's performance post-COVID.

Fees

Another negative consideration is the fund's relatively high fee burden, with an expense ratio of 0.6%. For long-term holders, this results in a substantial loss of capital to fees. For example, over a ten year holding period, around 5.8% of total return is lost to fees. Over a twenty year period, the figure is 11.3%. It's also important to consider that, given the high fee ratio, almost the entirety of the fund's ~0.8% dividend yield is consumed by these charges.

Upside Risks

Clearly, there is the possibility that my perspective on the industry is excessively negative. In the short-term at least, the back-log in terms of deliveries of commercial aircraft is a positive consideration for passenger airlines, since there is less supply to serve the ever-increasing demand for travel.

In the long-term, however, my serious concerns remain, and I do think this outweighs any short-term positives which could occur, such as a decline in oil prices or artificial intelligence allowing operators to become more efficient and expand their margins.

Conclusion

Modern passenger aircraft are a clear technological marvel. Unfortunately though, the business of operating them is much less marvelous, demonstrated by the razor-thin margins of the four largest U.S. airlines which represent the core focus of the JETS fund. In consideration of the significant possibility of sudden and unpredictable disruption to the airline industry in the coming decades, I consider them to be unsuitable for long-term investors, and am compelled to award a strong sell rating to the ETF.

data.title: Visa's Growth Is Great, But Doesn't Make It A Buy
data.description:
When it comes to the payment processing industry, one company that absolutely should not be ignored is Visa Inc. (V). The firm is a giant in the space and has a great track record of growth. Unfortunately, the market has reflected that in the company's share price, with the stock often trading at rather lofty multiples. But for those who want a high-quality company that should do reasonably well in the long run, this is certainly a prospect to keep in mind. In my last article about it, published in September of 2025, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. This was based on the overall quality of the firm and its growth. Since then, the stock has outperformed the market slightly, going up 6.2% compared to the 5% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Having said that, since I originally rated it a ‘hold’ way back in November of 2023, the stock has underperformed some, rising by 42.8% compared to the 52.4% that the market experienced.

Despite this long-term underperformance, this is one of those plays that, I believe, makes sense for those who want stability and safety. The overall growth of the company should continue, especially with recent innovations that management has made. And the overall fundamental health of the company remains intact. Given these factors, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate makes the most sense to me.

A Great Business

Over the years, I have come to view Visa as a great business. But there is a big difference between a company being great and its share price making sense. The fact of the matter is that the company is great because financial performance continues to chug along nicely. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This is the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $10.72 billion. That's 11.5% above the $9.62 billion the company reported a year earlier.

The company has benefited from multiple drivers during that time. For instance, the number of cards that it has outstanding hit 4.9 billion for that quarter. That's up materially from the 4.6 billion that it had outstanding the previous year. In fact, even though it was modest, the company saw an increase in market share associated with cards. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. This particular chart looks at market share amongst three big players in the industry, with those being Visa, Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AXP). Market share inched up ever so slightly from 56.32% to 56.40%. I know that's not much of an increase. But any sort of growth in a large, mature, and hyper-competitive space, is great to see.

There are other metrics that we should be looking at also. For instance, year over year, overall payments volume for the company increased about 9%. But this requires some unpacking. According to the data provided, the company processed $4.375 trillion worth of transactions during the quarter. That's up from the $4.249 trillion processed the previous year. The growth in the number of cards certainly helped to facilitate this. By comparison, in its latest quarter, Mastercard was only responsible for $2.747 trillion. American Express was far behind, totaling $479.2 billion. Unfortunately, because of the more rapid growth experienced by the competition, specifically with Mastercard experiencing an increase of 9.8% year over year, Visa’s share of transaction value contracted from 59.08% to 57.56%. But it still is the big player amongst the three.

Transaction data is also valuable for us to assess. Because this increase in processed volume was not just from customers spending more. It was actually from an increase in the number of transactions. This makes sense considering the rise in the number of cards outstanding. The company processed a mind-boggling 82.1 billion transactions for the quarter. That's up from the 79.3 billion transactions reported the previous year. And for 2025 in its entirety, the 272 billion transactions of the company processed ended up coming in 8.1% above the 251.5 billion reported for 2024.

As revenue for the company increases, you would normally expect profitability to rise. As the chart above illustrates, this was the case for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. But as the first chart in this article shows, profits can be somewhat lumpy. In fact, net income dropped from $5.32 billion in the final quarter of 2024 to $5.09 billion the same time of 2025. The good news is that this drop was driven primarily by $903 million in litigation costs that the company incurred. This compares to just $10 million reported the previous year. Unfortunately, litigation has become rather expensive for the company as of late, but in theory it should be a one-time thing. For 2025 in its entirety, the company reported litigation charges of $2.56 billion. That's massively above the $462 million reported a year earlier.

This rise in costs was driven by additional accruals of $2.2 billion that the company incurred in order to address claims involving the interchange multidistrict litigation situation that it has going on. This is essentially an antitrust case that the company is contending with, whereby it has been alleged that it and competitor Mastercard illegally fixed interchange fees and restricted competition. The company has long-faced these charges. In fact, even though it was partially overturned, back in 2012, a $7.25 billion settlement was reached regarding these types of claims. And in 2024, a judge rejected a $30 billion settlement offer. But this is still an ongoing issue, with some estimates pegging the ultimate nominal value of this litigation to be $38 billion.

Despite these problems, the company is still doing quite well. In the final quarter, it is true that operating cash flow of $6.24 billion fell short of the $6.66 billion reported the previous year. But when we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $9.32 billion to $9.62 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business totaled $7.37 billion for the quarter, which was up from the $6.63 billion the company reported the previous year.

If we use the historical results that the company achieved for 2024 and 2025, we can see in the chart below how the company is priced compared to the same two competitors that I stacked it up against earlier in this article. Although the trading multiples for Visa are rather lofty, they are in line with the competition. The fact of the matter is that the payment processing industry has a tendency to trade at high multiples anyways. So to see the company at least come in and around the same levels as these other firms is encouraging in and of itself.

Long term, I do believe in the operational opportunities that the company offers. Management continues to come out with positive developments and good news. On December 17th of last year, for instance, the company announced a new strategic collaboration with Akamai Technologies (AKAM), which is a cybersecurity and cloud computing company, whereby the firms will tap into the power of agentic commerce. For context, this refers to AI software agents in the dew shopping on behalf of people. This includes things like browsing, comparing items, and even purchasing products using little to no human involvement. It is essentially an AI shopping assistant. This particular arrangement will involve Visa’s Trusted Agent Protocol, which is used to authenticate AI agents and link them back to the real consumers that they represent. If done correctly, this will empower merchants to trust and distinguish legitimate AI agents from malicious bots before any transaction is ultimately completed.

Also in December, the company launched stablecoin settlement in the US. This is an opportunity that I discussed about for the company in a previous article. In essence, the company mentioned that it was launching its USDC settlement in the US, which would give issuers the benefit of faster funds movement over blockchains, including across weekends and holidays. This is a big opportunity for the company, and management has long been working on growing into this market. Already as of November 30th, the company accomplished monthly stablecoin settlement volume in excess of $3.5 billion on an annual run rate basis. So it is already doing well in this industry. Literally, a day before that, on December 15th, management announced the launch of its Stablecoin Advisory Practice, which is a new value added service provided by the business that will provide actionable insights and recommendations for banks, financial technology firms, merchants, and other businesses, when it comes to things like market fit, strategy, and implementation in the stablecoin space. Seeing as how the stablecoin industry is already worth more than $250 billion and is certain to grow further, this could be an attractive and high margin revenue source for the company.

There have been other updates as well that fall outside of business development and innovation. On December 23rd of last year, for instance, the company revealed data regarding holiday sales. They said that spending rose 4.2% year over year, with e-commerce leading the way with a 7.8% jump. Surprisingly, electronics were an area of growth for consumers, with spending up 5.8% year over year. Although e-commerce sales continue to grow, according to management, 73% of all holiday payment volume in the US is still in physical stores. I honestly find that number hard to believe. Also, it's worth noting that other countries saw strength as well, with spending up 5% in Australia, 4.4% in Canada, 7.9% in South Africa, and 3.6% in the UK. All of this bodes well for the company, considering its business model.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that Visa is a fantastic company. Long term, I expect it to continue growing. But this does not mean that it makes sense to buy into. The stock is rather lofty, and looks more or less fairly valued compared to other firms in the space. And even though I expect continued business development and innovation initiatives to bode well for the firm, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: BYD Is A Key Part Of The World Shifting Towards BEVs
data.description:
Introduction

Per my August article, BYD Company Limited (BYDDY) is seeing growth in terms of selling new energy vehicles (“NEVs”) overseas. This continues as the December CPCA National Passenger Market Report says BYD exported 131,637 units in December. Unlike Tesla (TSLA), who only sells battery electric vehicles (“BEVs”), BYD also sells plug-in hybrid vehicles (“PHEVs”). NEVs include both BEVs and PHEVs.

Per the December National Passenger Market Report, China accounts for 68% of global NEV share for the first 11 months of 2025. This includes 64.3% of the BEV share and 76.4% of the PHEV share. As such, if we want to analyze the global NEV market then we have to look at China and if we want to analyze China’s NEV market then we have to look at BYD.

BYD saw BEV sales climb almost 28% from 1,764,992 units in 2024 up to 2,256,714 units in 2025 per the December CPCA Manufacturer Ranking Report. It's impressive how they sold 491,722 more BEVs in a year when sentiment in some markets was bearish on BEVs.

My thesis is that BYD is a key part of the world shifting to BEVs.

At the time of this writing, RMB 1,000 is about $143.32.

The Numbers

On a global basis, Tesla was slightly ahead of BYD with BEVs in 2024 but everything changed in 2025. It was a year where BYD sold nearly half a million more BEVs worldwide while Tesla saw a drop of more than 150 thousand units. Combined, BYD and Geely (GELYY) sold nearly a million more BEVs in 2025 than they did in 2024 while Tesla saw units fall:

Despite headlines about flat BEV markets in parts of the U.S. and Europe, BEV sales have been increasing substantially worldwide and much of this is because of BYD and China. Per the January 2025 CPCA National Passenger Market Report, global auto sales for 2024 were 90.6 million units and BEVs had 11.4% share while PHEVs had 6.3% share implying unit numbers of 10.3 million and 5.7 million, respectively. For 2025 global units, we only have the first 11 months at this time from the December 2025 CPCA National Passenger Market Report. For the first 11 months of 2025, global auto sales were 87.66 million. The global BEV penetration rate rose significantly to 15.3% while the PHEV penetration rate went up slightly to 7.9% which implies 11-month unit figures of 13.4 million and 6.9 million, respectively. In just the first 11 months of 2025, the global BEV unit sub total beat the full year total for 2024 by over 3 million units!

BYD is by far the largest BEV wholesaler in China but many other OEMs are copying some of their techniques and increasing their numbers. Above we showed the global units for Tesla but below we only show only the Tesla China wholesale units:
